response:
## Results

We evaluated ten experimental variants of our proposed method on the validation set, reporting mean accuracy and standard deviation over five random seeds for each configuration. Among these, the baseline—corresponding to the model without our proposed component—achieved an accuracy of 67.8% (±0.6). Notably, one of our configurations, variant_08, achieved a substantial improvement, reaching 70.4% (±0.5) accuracy. This represents an absolute gain of +2.6 percentage points over the baseline, corresponding to a relative improvement of 3.8%. The low standard deviation further indicates that the gains from variant_08 are consistent across different random initializations.

The remaining variants yielded accuracy values that were either comparable to or slightly below the baseline. This comprehensive evaluation demonstrates that while not all instantiations of the proposed method result in improvements, the correct configuration—corresponding to variant_08—delivers a clear and robust performance boost. Importantly, the improvements of variant_08 are achieved without any increase in model complexity or inference cost, underscoring the practical benefits of our approach.

Overall, these results highlight the importance of careful design choices when incorporating the proposed component. When properly instantiated, our method delivers state-of-the-art performance on the validation benchmark, as evidenced by the significant and reliable gains of variant_08 over the strong baseline. This demonstrates the efficacy and potential impact of our approach for practitioners seeking to maximize accuracy on this task.
